Service Connection: The VA's determination that your disability was caused by — or made worse by — your military service. Without service connection, there is no compensation. The three legs: (1) a current diagnosed condition, (2) an in-service event, injury, or illness, and (3) a nexus linking the two.
Nexus: The medical link between your current condition and your service. Usually established by a medical opinion stating it is 'at least as likely as not' (50% or greater probability) that your condition is related to service. A nexus letter from a qualified provider must be honest, specific, and based on your actual records — a vague or templated letter carries little weight.
Nexus Letter: A written medical opinion from a doctor connecting your condition to service. A strong, legitimate one: reviews your service treatment records and claims file, cites relevant medical literature, explains the reasoning, and uses the 'at least as likely as not' standard. It documents a real connection — it never invents one.
C&P Exam: Compensation & Pension exam. A VA-ordered examination to evaluate your claimed condition. It is not treatment — it documents severity for rating purposes. Prepare by knowing your own medical history and describing your symptoms accurately, including how bad your worst days are. Never exaggerate and never minimize: both distort your rating, and exaggeration in a federal claim is a crime.
Secondary Condition: A condition caused or aggravated by an already service-connected condition (e.g., a knee injury altering your gait and damaging your hip, or medication side effects). Secondary claims still require a current diagnosis and a medical nexus to the primary condition.
Effective Date: The date your benefits start — usually the date the VA received your claim or intent to file. This is why filing an Intent to File (VA Form 21-0966) early matters: it locks in your date while you gather evidence for up to a year.
Intent to File: VA Form 21-0966. Tells the VA you plan to file, preserving your effective date for up to one year while you assemble your claim properly.
Schedular Rating: Your rating under the VA Schedule for Rating Disabilities (38 CFR Part 4) — the standard percentage system from 0% to 100% in 10% steps.
TDIU: Total Disability based on Individual Unemployability. Pays at the 100% rate even if your combined rating is lower, when service-connected conditions prevent you from maintaining substantially gainful employment. Generally requires one condition at 60%+, or a combined 70%+ with one condition at 40%+. Requires honest documentation of your actual work history and limitations.
SMC: Special Monthly Compensation. Additional compensation above the 100% rate for specific severe circumstances — loss or loss of use of limbs or organs, need for aid and attendance, being housebound, and others. Levels run from SMC-K through SMC-R/S/T.
VA Math: Combined ratings are not added — each new rating applies only to your remaining capacity. 50% + 50% is 75%, which rounds to 80%, not 100%. Use the calculator in this app to see the exact steps.
Bilateral Factor: When disabilities affect both arms, both legs, or paired muscles, the VA combines those ratings and adds 10% of the result before combining with everything else (38 CFR 4.26). Small, but it can tip you over a rounding threshold.
Buddy Statement: A lay statement (VA Form 21-10210 / formerly 21-4138) from someone with firsthand knowledge — a fellow service member, spouse, or friend — describing what they personally witnessed. It must be truthful; it is signed under penalty of law.
Presumptive Condition: Conditions the VA presumes are service-connected for certain groups (e.g., burn pit / PACT Act exposures, Agent Orange, Gulf War illness). You still need a current diagnosis, but the nexus is presumed if you meet the service criteria.
Claims File (C-File): The VA's complete file on you — every record, exam, and decision. You can request a copy. Reviewing it is one of the highest-value things you can do: many denials trace to evidence the VA never had.
Rating Decision: The VA's written decision on your claim, listing each condition, the rating assigned, the effective date, and crucially the reasoning. Read the reasoning — it tells you exactly what evidence was missing if you were denied.
Deferred: The VA needs more evidence or an exam before deciding that issue. Not a denial.
VSO: Veterans Service Officer — accredited representatives (DAV, VFW, American Legion, county VSOs, etc.) who help you file for free. This app prepares you to work with one effectively; it does not replace one.
Concurrent Receipt (CRDP/CRSC): Rules governing whether you can receive both military retired pay and VA disability compensation. CRDP applies broadly at 50%+ for 20-year retirees; CRSC covers combat-related conditions. Handled through DFAS, not just the VA.
Aggravation: When service made a pre-existing condition permanently worse beyond its natural progression. This is its own valid path to service connection.
